Enzymes
Timer 13:00
Passage 1 (Questions 1–7)

Investigators studying hepatic nitrogen handling used a simplified three-enzyme model based on the urea-cycle sequence. In their model, enzyme 1 converts ornithine to citrulline, enzyme 2 converts citrulline to argininosuccinate, and enzyme 3 converts argininosuccinate to arginine. Figure 1 shows the Michaelis–Menten relationship between initial velocity and substrate concentration, and Equation 1 shows the Eadie–Hofstee form of the same kinetics. The researchers emphasized a common error: a lower KM suggests higher apparent affinity, but it does not by itself prove that an enzyme controls pathway flux under cellular conditions.

Table 1 lists the KM values measured for the three enzymes with their own substrates. In separate basal-state lysate assays, the maximal velocities were 120 nmol/min for enzyme 1, 90 nmol/min for enzyme 2, and 30 nmol/min for enzyme 3. After glucagon stimulation, enzyme 3 expression increased threefold without changing its KM, whereas enzymes 1 and 2 were unchanged. In pulse-chase experiments, the relevant intermediate concentrations were typically about 5 × 10−5 M between meals and rose to about 5 × 10−4 M after a high-protein meal.

The authors also tested inhibitor Y, which binds free enzyme 2 but not the enzyme–substrate complex. Students were warned not to assume that the lowest-KM enzyme must always be rate-limiting. At low substrate concentration, an enzyme can still contribute little overall flux if its Vmax is small. Likewise, changes in KM and Vmax must be interpreted together when evaluating how a mutation or inhibitor will affect an assay.

Figure 1 Reaction velocity plotted in relation to substrate concentration.
Equation 1 Eadie–Hofstee form used to relate V0, Vmax, and KM.
Table 1 Various KM values for the three enzymes.

Use this passage the same way you would approach a research-style MCAT enzyme question set:

  • Start with the figure: Figure 1 reminds you that when [S] = KM, the enzyme is operating at Vmax/2. When [S] is far below KM, velocity is much more sensitive to substrate changes; when [S] is far above KM, the curve begins to level off.
  • Then use the table numerically: compare the meal-related substrate values in the passage (5 × 10−5 M and 5 × 10−4 M) with the KM values in Table 1. That tells you which enzymes are operating far below, near, or above their KM.
  • Do not confuse affinity with pathway control: the passage explicitly warns that the smallest KM does not automatically mean the largest pathway flux. Bring in the basal and glucagon-stimulated Vmax values before deciding which step is the bottleneck.
  • Translate the inhibitor clue carefully: inhibitor Y binds free enzyme 2, not the enzyme–substrate complex. That fits competitive inhibition, which changes apparent KM while leaving Vmax unchanged. In the Eadie–Hofstee form, that means the slope changes but the y-intercept stays at Vmax.
Question 1 of 7
